data.title: Stock market's big battle this week: Yields 1, AI 0
data.description:
With one of the world's biggest tech convictions kicking off this week, there was potential for a Wall Street rally based on the artificial intelligence (AI) updates provided by major companies at the event.

Instead, the Consumer Electronics Show (CES) was overshadowed by hot economic data that led to a recalibration of Federal Reserve interest rate cuts and a major selloff in bonds. It essentially set up a battle between AI and U.S. Treasury yields that saw the latter win. Wall Street suffered, with the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) slipping into negative territory for the new year.

AI-market leader Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD) grabbed some of the biggest headlines at CES, with both chipmakers unveiling their widely anticipated latest offerings. Other companies that appeared at the event included Marvell Technology (MRVL), Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), and Mobileye (MBLY).

But CES found itself relegated to the sidelines, as headlines during the week were instead dominated by labor market and inflation indicators that an easing Fed does not want to see. U.S. Treasury yields surged - the 10-year yield (US10Y) posted a weekly advance of 17 basis points - as bonds were dumped, while equities were hammered. The S&P 500 (SP500) Technology sector was -3.10% for the week, with the Magnificent 7 club down -1.7%. Nvidia (NVDA) shed -5.9%.

Higher rates can put pressure on stock valuations, as corporations need to generate better earnings to grab investor interest. For technology companies in particular, valuations are generally based on the perception that a large chunk of their revenue and profits will come much further down the line. Thus, higher rates can make the present value of the future cash flows much less valuable.

With the Fed starting its rate-cutting cycle in September last year, higher rates were not seen as a concern. But, following the strong economic data recently and remarks from central bank speakers, the outlook for rate cuts has dramatically changed. Traders now see only two cuts in 2025.

For technology companies, the dialing back of rate cut odds represents a headwind. The S&P 500 (SP500) Technology sector was the second-best performer among the 11 sectors in 2024, advancing a whopping +35.69%, while its accompanying Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLK) gained +20.80%.

"The rise in global interest rates is a double-edged sword. Strong momentum in the global expansion at the turn of the year should raise real yields and reduce the need for policy ease. However, rising term-premia, reflecting both inflation and public finance concerns, is contributing to a broad rise in global borrowing costs," JPMorgan's Bruce Kasman said on Friday.

One of the other major drivers behind the rise in U.S. Treasury yields has also been uncertainty and concerns over the effects of incoming President Donald Trump's policies. His promise of levying more tariffs is already seen as inflationary by analysts and economists.

"An acceleration in economic activity occurred following Election Day as the prospects of stateside pro-growth policies boosted business confidence. But the possible inflationary impacts of adversarial trade postures and immigration reform lurk in the background, especially since goods drove the disinflation train from 2022 and the nation has a labor shortage, which, if worsened, could push up wage bills," José Torres, senior economist at Interactive Brokers, said on Friday.

"Indeed, non-inflationary growth is key to a successful Trump 2.0 administration, as it would boost stock prices and keep the Fed off the backs of the market and economy," Torres added.

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest linked to the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) and to the technology sector: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

data.title: Insurers gain Wall Street's vote of confidence, with caveats
data.description:
Wall Street holds a cautiously optimistic view on the insurance sector for 2025, favoring insurance brokers while taking a bearish stance on reinsurers.

The insurance space generally saw improved underwriting results and benefited from higher investment income driven by higher interest rates, though some faced pressure from catastrophic losses and inflation in claim costs. Insurance stocks (NYSEARCA:KIE) and the S&P 500 Index each posted a ~24% climb in 2024, trailing the broader financial sector's 28% advance.

UBS analyst Brian Meredith is particularly concerned about the setup for property and casualty insurers this year, owing to a likely peak in underwriting margins for most companies amid "higher loss picks from elevated tort inflation," he wrote in a recent note to clients.

Evercore ISI's David Motemaden upgraded P&C insurance stocks Allstate (ALL), Aon (AON) and Willis Towers Watson (WTW) on the view that the market hasn't yet recognized the growth potential for the three stocks.

One of the main risks for the insurance industry, however, is the notable increase in losses from global natural catastrophes, which usually lead to a spike in claims, higher reinsurance costs and dampen underwriting profitability. Insured losses from the ongoing wildfires in Southern California are expected to be at least $20B, J.P. Morgan had estimated Thursday, pointing to Allstate (ALL), Travelers (TRV) and Chubb (CB) as the most exposed carriers.

Elsewhere in the insurance world, UBS's Meredith noted he still favors personal lines in the near-term, "with margins stable to improving and accelerating growth." Also, "we are more positive on insurance brokers given more reasonable relative valuations and expectations of mid to high single digit organic growth with margin expansion in 2025. We are negative reinsurance with increasing capital levels driving more competitive pricing and lower returns (albeit from very attractive levels)."

His top insurance picks into the Q4 2024 earnings season include Marsh & McLennan Companies (MMC) and Kemper Corp. (KMPR).

Meanwhile, J.P. Morgan analyst Jimmy Bhullar remains bullish on life insurance stocks, having named Global Life (GL) his top pick and raised his rating recommendation on Principal Financial Group (PFG) to Overweight from Neutral.

"The favorable macro backdrop (strong equity market, higher interest rates, tight labor market), insurers’ healthy balance sheets and flexibility for share buybacks, and current valuations will enable life insurance stocks to outperform further," Bhullar wrote in a Jan. 7 note.

However, he downgraded Unum Group (UNM) to Neutral from Overweight, as the stock's recent outperformance adversely impacted its risk-reward profile.

Seeking Alpha's Quant system gives the highest ranking among publicly-traded insurers to Kingstone Companies (KINS), followed by Allstate (ALL), eHealth (EHTH), Root (ROOT), Travelers (TRV) and Mercury General (MCY).

On auto insurance carriers, Seeking Alpha analyst The CrickAnt is waiting for a "more reasonable valuation" to invest in Progressive (PGR), which he considers a “Geico without Warren Buffett."

data.title: Executive reshuffles: META, LUV, CCI and WPM
data.description:
Market watchers, throughout the week, observed several Wall Street executives stepping up for new roles as part of leadership reshuffling.

Meanwhile, some companies such as Meta Platforms, Southwest Airlines and Crown Castle added new positions to steer growth prospects.

Here are some of the recent management changes that took place during the week:

Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) has bolstered its board of directors with three new members, most notably adding UFC CEO Dana White, a crucial figure in modern MMA sports and a prolific campaigner for incoming president Donald Trump.

Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) said that Executive Vice President & CFO Tammy Romo and Chief Administration Officer Linda Rutherford will retire from their positions effective April 1, 2025.

Crown Castle (NYSE:CCI) said that Daniel K. Schlanger will, effective March 31, 2025, cease serving as Executive Vice President and CFO.

Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) said CFO Gary Brown will step down from the role, and Vice President of Finance Vincent Lau has been appointed a successor. Brown's departure is effective March 31.

American International Group (AIG) has announced the appointment of Christopher Flatt as Chief Underwriting Officer, effective immediately.

NNN REIT (NNN) has announced that Kevin B. Habicht, executive vice president, CFO, assistant secretary, treasurer and the Co.'s principal financial officer, will retire and step down as a member of the board effective March 31, 2025.

Ulta Beauty (ULTA) CEO Dave Kimbell has announced his retirement and will step down as the Co.’s chief executive and board member. He will be replaced by the company’s COO Kecia Steelman.

The Hershey (HSY) has announced that Michele Buck, Chairman of the Board of Directors, President and CEO, has informed the board of her intention to retire from the Co. effective June 30, 2026.

Organovo Holdings (ONVO) has announced that it had appointed Norman Staskey as CFO.

Coca‑Cola Consolidated (COKE) said that Executive Vice President and CFO, Scott Anthony, will retire effective March 31, 2025. The company also announced that Matthew J. Blickley will assume the role of Executive Vice President and CFO, effective April 1, 2025.

Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) has announced the appointment of James Whitlinger as executive vice president and CFO, effective Jan. 1, 2025.

Inseego (INSG) announced the appointment of Juho Sarvikas as CEO and a Director on the Co.’s Board of Directors, effective immediately.

Health and wellness services company XWELL (XWEL) has announced that Ian Brown has been appointed CFO, effective Jan. 6.

data.title: The top 10 private equity data center deals of 2024
data.description:
The rise of large language models and other artificial intelligence (AI) applications requiring massive amounts of computing power have fuelled the rampant rise in demand for demand centers. The private equity world also took notice of this trend in 2024, and shelled out billions of dollars on data center deals.

According to data from research firm PitchBook, Blackstone (BX) engineered the biggest data center deal last year in leading a consortium that scooped up Australia's AirTrunk for A$24B ($14.75B). Other high-profile private equity (PE) names such as KKR (KKR), Silver Lake, and Starwood Capital Group also jumped into the action.

Here are the 10 largest PE data center deals of 2024, as compiled by PitchBook:

See the following breakdown for further details on the three biggest deals:

  • AirTrunk: Founded in Australia in 2015, AirTrunk opened and expanded hyperscale data centers in the Oceanian country from 2017 to 2019. It received funding from Goldman Sachs in 2017, followed by another investment round led by Macquarie in 2020 that valued AirTrunk at $1.84B. Founder and top boss Robin Khuda called the Blackstone deal "the biggest milestone in the AirTrunk journey so far."
  • Vantage Data Centers: Launched in 2010 after acquiring a fully operational data center in Santa Clara, Calif. with an investment from Silver Lake Partners. Vantage was acquired by DigitalBridge (DBRG) in 2017. Both firms tied up to lead the $9.2B equity investment in Vantage last year, at a time when the company has now expanded to own or control more than 25 sites in North America and EMEA totaling over three GW of expected capacity.
  • DataBank: Founded in 2005 in Dallas, Texas, the company has today expanded its footprint to more than 65 data centers in over 27 markets. DataBank was acquired by a group of investors led by DigitalBridge (DBRG) in 2016. The 2024 $2B equity raise in DataBank was led by AustralianSuper, Australia’s largest superannuation fund.

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest tied to the technology sector, AI and robotics: (VGT), (NYSEARCA:XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), (RSPT), (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Can 2025 J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference spark biotech M&A?
data.description:
As top pharma executives arrive in San Francisco, CA, to attend the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference next week, biotech dealmaking will be in focus as the annual event usually coincides with major M&A announcements.

With Big Pharma facing significant patent cliffs in 2028 and 2029, M&A could be a major healthcare theme in 2025 amid expectations of a friendlier regulatory backdrop and given the potential acquirers' stronger financial position, J.P. Morgan analyst Chris Schott said.

"As I think about major themes for 2025, M&A really is one of the areas we're going to be paying attention to," Schott said, adding, "We've got balance sheets across the sector in a much better place than they've been."

"We're seeing the biotech sector continue to produce innovative medicines that, quite honestly, can be maximized in the hands of a larger partner or being acquired."

In the lead-up to the event, Big Pharma has already indicated their dealmaking prowess, with Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and GSK (NYSE:GSK) reportedly looking to acquire privately owned biotechs Scorpion Therapeutics and IDRx for $2.5B and $1B, respectively.

Meanwhile, Biogen (NASDAQ:BIIB) has already offered $7.22 per share to acquire a stake that it currently doesn't own in depression drug developer Sage Therapeutics (SAGE) as part of a non-binding agreement.

Ahead of the event, Seeking Alpha Investing Group Leader Edmund Ingham named Regeneron (REGN), Vertex Pharma (VRTX), Merck (MRK), and Biogen (NASDAQ:BIIB) among drugmakers looking to announce major M&A deals in the near term.

"I could see Vertex (VRTX) acquiring Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) and Merck acquiring Moderna (MRNA) in 2025. They have the funds to do so, and their interests are already strategically aligned," Ingham wrote.

Meanwhile, SA Investing Group Leader Terry Chrisomalis added Pfizer (PFE) to the list, noting that the COVID-19 vaccine maker needs to establish a beachhead in the obesity space and boost its stagnating topline after the pandemic-era fortunes. "Thus, it's my belief that if this Big Pharma wants to compete in this space, it's likely going to have to shop for an obesity drug development biotech for an M&A transaction," Chrisomalis added.

According to him, Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) is a top acquisition target as the company has a strong pipeline focused on obesity and a liver disease called Metabolic-Dysfunction Associated Steatohepatitis (MASH).

"With Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) doing well with VK2809 in targeting the large MASH market and the possible FDA approval of VK2735 for the ever-growing obesity market, I truly believe that this biotech is next in line to be acquired," he said.

According to Edmund Ingham, Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL), Summit Therapeutics (SMMT), Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), uniQure (QURE), Scholar Rock (SRRK), and Novocure (NVCR) could be prime M&A candidates.

Apart from Viking (VKTX), the analyst named Structure Therapeutics (GPCR) and Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN) among other potential targets in the obesity space.

However, Citi analyst Geoff Meacham doesn't expect significant M&A transactions at this year's JPM event. "With broader uncertainties around Trump admin elects and Biopharma M&A commentary, we do not expect major M&A activities next week," Meacham wrote.

"We suspect the focus from management teams will be on HC policy and what the first 90 days of Trump 2.0 could bring to the biopharma space."

data.title: Treasury secretary-nominee Scott Bessent to divest assets to avoid conflicts: report
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump’s nominee for Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent, will divest from dozens of funds, trust, and investments to take the post if he is confirmed, The New York Times reported Saturday, citing Bessent’s financial disclosure forms.

According to the report, the plans were released Saturday along with an ethics agreement and financial disclosure forms that Bessent filed ahead of his confirmation hearing before the Senate Finance Committee on Thursday.

The Times said the documents show Bessent’s assets and investments appear to be worth more than $700M.

Bessent was a hedge fund manager when Trump picked him for the post.

Bessent reportedly said in a letter to the Treasury Department’s ethics office that he would close investment Key Square Capital Management, which he founded, and resign from his Bessent-Freeman Family Foundation as well as Rockefeller University, where he had been the chair of its investment committee.

The Times reported that Bessent’s financial disclosure form showed he owned up to $25M in North Dakota farmland, which earned money from soybean and corn production. A proopety in the Bahamas was valued at up to $25M. He put his Charleston, South Carolina mansion up for sale for $22.5M.

He is also selling off an investment in Bitcoin exchange-traded fund, an account that trades the Chinese yuan, and his stake in conservative publisher All Seasons, the report said, adding that he has a more than $50M line of credit with Goldman Sachs.

The Trump transition team did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

More on the economy and markets

  • Surprise Strength In Labor Market May Keep Rates Higher
  • December Jobs Report: Busting The Fed's Labor Market Assessment
  • Catalyst Watch: Bank earnings, oil updates, powerhouse conferences in retail and healthcare
  • Stocks end lower as hot jobs data sparks recalibration of rate cut bets

data.title: Key deals this week: FuboTV - Disney, Constellation Energy, Eli Lilly, Phillips 66, Intercontinental Exchange and more
data.description:
The mergers and acquisitions market has already seen a flurry of deals at the start of 2025 across sectors. Here's a look at some of the key transactions:

Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG) hit an all-time high yesterday after agreeing to buy privately-held natural gas and geothermal company Calpine for $26.6 billion.

Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) is in advanced discussions to purchase cancer-focused biotech Scorpion Therapeutics for as much as $2.5 billion.

data.title: Data center infrastructure revenue to reach $282B in 2024, capacity to triple by 2030
data.description:
Total data center infrastructure equipment revenues probably reached $282B in 2024, and hyperscale data center capacity is expected to triple by 2030, according to analytics firm Synergy Research Group.

The artificial intelligence (AI) boom has led to massive demand for high-powered graphics processing units ((GPUs)), which in turn has led to major investments in data centers to support those chips.

“GPUs and generative AI systems lit a fire under the market in 2024, resulting in record growth rates for the industry. While the ongoing success of public cloud has been the main driving force behind data center investments for well over a decade now, no-one imagined a 2024 market for data center gear reaching over $280B,” John Dinsdale, a chief analyst at Synergy Research Group, said on Tuesday.

See the following graphic shared by Synergy:

Overall worldwide spend on data center hardware and software in 2024 probably grew 34% Y/Y, Synergy said, primarily due to a 48% jump in spending on public cloud infrastructure.

Market Leaders of 2024

Synergy's figures are based on actual data for the first three quarters of 2024, along with the analytics firm's forecast for Q4.

Excluding original design manufacturers, Synergy found that Dell (DELL) was the overall leader in server and storage segment revenues. Meanwhile, Cisco (CSCO) emerged as the leader in the networking segment, while Microsoft (MSFT) featured prominently in the rankings "due to its position in server OS and virtualization applications."

"Nvidia (NVDA) now features heavily as a supplier both to other system vendors and directly to service providers. Outside of these five, the other leading vendors in the market are HPE (HPE), Super Micro (SMCI), Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY)(OTCPK:LNVGF), VMware (AVGO), Huawei, IBM (IBM) and Arista Networks (ANET)," Synergy said.

'Supercharged' Generative AI

Synergy Research believes that the average capacity of hyperscale data centers to be opened over the next four years will be almost double that of current capacity.

“The number of operational hyperscale data centers continues to grow inexorably, having doubled over the past five years. That installed base will continue to grow, but the most marked change in the market is the ever-increasing capacity of data centers that are being brought online," Synergy's John Dinsdale said on Thursday.

"The trend has always been for the critical IT load of hyperscale data centers to grow in size over time, but generative AI technology and services are power hungry and have supercharged that trend. Meanwhile, as the average IT load of individual data centers ramps up, the number of operational hyperscale data centers will continue to steadily grow," the analytics firm added.

See the following graphic shared by Synergy:

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest tied to the technology sector, AI and robotics: (VGT), (NYSEARCA:XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), (RSPT), (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (GINN), (IRBO), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Bulls vs. Bears: Is Palantir stock a Sell as ARK Invest cuts stake?
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR), one of the best-performing tech stocks in 2024, fell roughly 13% over the past week, underperforming the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP: IND), amid a ~$15M stake sale from ARK Invest.

After a series of similar sales over the past two months, does Cathie Wood-led investment firm’s latest decision to offload roughly 196.7K shares in Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) indicate a bearish outlook for the Denver, Colorado-based data analytics platform?

The Bulls

“Palantir's strong government and military contracts, particularly with the U.S. Army and AI initiatives, highlight its competitive advantages and growth prospects,” argued SA analyst Tangerine Tan Capital in “Palantir Stock: Remains Attractive For Those Looking To Invest For The Long Term.” “The biggest risk is share dilution; however, PLTR's unique position and exceptional AI capabilities make it a compelling long-term investment.”

“Despite a high forward P/E ratio, Palantir's exceptional EPS growth justifies its valuation, with potential for additional upside,” added SA analyst Noah's Arc Capital Management in “Palantir: Winning The Race To AGI.” “I think Palantir should be trading at an 800% premium to the sector median price-to-earnings ratio due to their 1125.08% projected premium to the sector median YoY EPS growth.”

The Bears

“Palantir's valuation is unsustainable, trading at extreme multiples, with a P/S ratio of 64.7x and forward P/E of 203x, far exceeding sector medians,” argued SA analyst Hataf Capital in “Palantir: Losing Momentum - Why 2025 Could Mark A Significant Downturn.” “Decelerating growth, massive insider selling, and extreme valuation metrics suggest a significant correction in 2025; I downgrade Palantir to Strong Sell.”

“Investors should be cautious as PLTR's valuation resembles overhyped COVID-era stocks,” added SA analyst The Software Side of Life in “Palantir: Applaud The Fundamentals, But Short Valuation.” “If the company were to guide 2025 revenue guidance similar to current estimates, the stock could potentially pull back significantly, as the valuation inherently implies upside to numbers.”

“Unfortunately, valuation analysis suggests that market participants have already fully priced in the potential of PLTR,” wrote SA analyst Selendis Research in “Palantir: Market Participants Have Already Fully Priced The Potential Of AI.” “Investors should avoid buying at the current price; those who already have positions should consider reducing.”

“If one ignores the valuation, there is indeed a lot to like about the stock. The company is GAAP profitable, has a net cash balance sheet, and revenue growth is accelerating,” opined SA Investing Group Leader Julian Lin in “Palantir: This Is Even Worse Than The 2021 Bubble.” “The stock's current valuation offers a nearly asymmetric return profile to the downside, warranting a “strong sell” rating,”

data.title: Iran selling oil stored in China to fund militias in Middle East: report
data.description:
Iran has shipped about 3M barrels from storage facilities in China to raise funds that may be spent on Tehran’s militia allies in the Middle East, The Wall Street Journal reported Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter.

According to the report, the oil is from stockpiles of at least 25M barrels Iran shipped to China in 2018, fearing new sanctions from the then-Trump administration, the people reportedly said.

The oil has been stored at ports in Dalian and Zhoushan, and two vessels recently set sail for the Dalian, the people were quoted as saying.

Beijing approved the shipments in December after discussions with Iranian officials in November and last month, the people reportedly said. The people reportedly added that it wasn’t the first time Tehran sought to drawn down the oil for sale, but it was the first time China gave its approval.

The Journal reported, one of the ships, the Madestar, departed Dalian earlier this month with 2M barrels of oil, and the second, the CH Billion, is thought to still be docked in Dalian and to be loaded with 700K barrels, the people said.

The people were quoted as saying Madestar ceased transmitting its location and intended route under the AIS international signal system earlier this month for three days while it was offshore Dalian and traveled to the port during that time. They reportedly said the ship headed to waters offshore South Korea to transfer oil to another vessel.

According to the people, CH Billion transmitted its position only briefly on Monday, the Journal reported.

Tehran could make up to $1B from the sale of the entire stockpiles, the people reportedly said, but it owes China about $1B in storage fees.

Some of the people were quoted as saying that Iran has earmarked the gains from selling the oil to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), which arms and funds Tehran’s militia allies around the Middle East.

One of the people said the U.S. has raised its concerns to China about funds going to the IRGC through its dealings with Iran.
